preFix.title: Paper clay Christmas ornament set
preFix.subtitle: Pressed and cut hanging ornaments for seasonal decoration
preFix.excerpt: A set of five Christmas ornaments cut from rolled paper clay: a star, a tree, a snowflake, a bauble, and a moon. Each is pressed with a texture stamp, pierced with a hanging hole, and finished with paint and varnish. No kiln required. Paper clay is used for its strength at thin sections and its clean impression-taking from stamps.
preFix.sourceNotes: Rolled and cut ornament technique derived from first principles. Standard air-dry craft practice.

content.text: ornaments is one of the most versatile seasonal projects in air-dry ceramics. The cellulose fibres in paper clay make it stronger than plain air-dry clay at the thin sections that Christmas shapes require. Stars, trees, and snowflakes all have points and narrow sections that would crack in plain clay. No kiln is required.

items.name: Cookie cutters (star, tree, circle, moon, snowflake)
items.substitution: Card templates and a craft knife

items.name: Pottery stamp or texture tile
items.substitution: Lace pressed directly into the clay

content.text: Roll paper clay between the slab guides to 5 mm thickness. The 5 mm thickness gives enough rigidity without the ornaments being too heavy on the tree. Lightly press a texture stamp or a scrap of lace over the surface before cutting the shapes. The texture impression carries through each cut shape.

Press the cookie cutters firmly and straight down through the clay, then lift the cutter free. If the shape sticks in the cutter, push it out gently from the back with a fingertip. Lay each cut shape on the board without moving it for the first hour of drying, so the thin points do not distort.

While the shapes are still soft, pierce a hole near the top of each with the needle tool. Twist the needle slowly to make a clean hole about 4 mm in diameter. The hole will close slightly as the clay dries; if it closes too much, re-open with the needle before the clay reaches
. Position the hole about 8 mm from the top edge so the ornament hangs from the correct point without tipping.
ornaments flat on the board for 24 hours, then turn them over so the underside dries evenly. Allow 24-48 hours total drying time before sanding.

Sand the edges lightly with fine sandpaper. Apply acrylic paint in chosen colours. Allow to dry. For a gilded look, apply a thin coat of gold acrylic over raised texture areas while the base colour is still slightly tacky. Once the paint is fully dry, apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish. Thread a 30 cm length of ribbon through the hanging hole and knot it at the back.
Stamp a name or word into each ornament with letter stamps for personalised gifts.
Press dried herb sprigs into the surface before cutting for botanical-imprint ornaments.
Make a set in different sizes from the same mould and hang them in a graduated cluster.

rows.fix: Paper clay handles this well, but drying in a cooler room or under a loose plastic sheet for the first 12 hours reduces cracking at points.
rows.cause: Clay dried too fast at thin sections
rows.problem: Points on stars and trees crack during drying

rows.fix: Check the holes after 2-3 hours and re-open with the needle tool if they are closing.
rows.cause: Clay shrinks as it dries, closing small holes
rows.problem: Hanging hole closes up during drying

rows.fix: Turn the ornaments over after the first 12 hours so both sides dry at the same rate.
rows.cause: The top surface dried faster than the underside
